title: MIDAS: Misalignment-based Data Augmentation Strategy for Imbalanced Multimodal Learning
abstract: Multimodal models often over-rely on dominant modalities, failing to achieve optimal performance. While prior work focuses on modifying training objectives or optimization procedures, data-centric solutions remain underexplored. We propose MIDAS, a novel data augmentation strategy that generates misaligned samples with semantically inconsistent cross-modal information, labeled using unimodal confidence scores to compel learning from contradictory signals. However, this confidence-based labeling can still favor the more confident modality. To address this within our misaligned samples, we introduce weak-modality weighting, which dynamically increases the loss weight of the least confident modality, thereby helping the model fully utilize weaker modality. Furthermore, when misaligned features exhibit greater similarity to the aligned features, these misaligned samples pose a greater challenge, thereby enabling the model to better distinguish between classes. To leverage this, we propose hard-sample weighting, which prioritizes such semantically ambiguous misaligned samples. Experiments on multiple multimodal classification benchmarks demonstrate that MIDAS significantly outperforms related baselines in addressing modality imbalance.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper presents a novel approach to addressing modality imbalance in multimodal learning by leveraging misaligned samples (synthetically generated examples that contain conflicting signals from different modalities). They posit that such samples force the model to use information from the non-dominant modality. They also propose two re-weighting mechanisms to further modality balance. One mechanism, weak-modality weighting, adjusts the contribution of the non-dominant modality in the loss computation based on the multi-model’s confidence. The second, hard-sample weighting increased the weight of samples where the substituted modality in the misaligned samples is similar to the original. Such samples are inherently harder for the model to learn, and hence provide a stronger signal for learning.

The proposed method is tested across several different multimodal datasets and shown to outperform other proposed methods for addressing modality imbalance. Ablations are performed to confirm that each each the three proposed components contributes meaningfully to the method’s efficacy.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

The paper is well written and easy to follow;  the mathematical details of the proposed method are clearly outlined.

The results are well presented, and plots are easy to follow. The proposed method shows moderate performance gains over baselines.

The presented work is on a highly relevant topic given the rise of multimodal learning across various settings. It addresses an important gap in existing work (i.e., the lack of data-level approaches for handling modality imbalance).

The use of misaligned samples as a training signal in this context is a novel and interesting approach

Weaknesses:

No analysis is presented on how well the proposed method performs on different modality combinations.  Fig. 2 is a bit overly complex, difficult to understand. The performance results are only presented for bimodal datasets

No analysis is presented to demonstrate that improved modality balance is the actual mechanism behind the observed performance gains.

QUESTIONS:
1. Do specific modality combinations or tasks make the proposed method more or less effective? This would provide greater insight into the generalizability of the approach.
2. Does the proposed method still offer performance benefits when the modalities are naturally balanced in their informational content? At the moment, it is unclear whether model performance could be degraded in such a scenario by training on misaligned samples.
3. Did you perform any experiments to specifically verify that the models actually do utilize information in both modalities more evenly after being trained with the proposed method? This seems important in validating the claim that MIDAS specifically addresses mobility imbalance.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper presents MIDAS, a data augmentation strategy to artificially generate multimodal misaligned samples, i.e., samples with semantically inconsistent information across different modalities, for example, a picture of a car paired with the caption of a different object. MIDAS uses unimodal classification confidence to combine modalities from different samples. The network is then trained to match the unimodal classification performance with the multimodal classifier, encouraging the model to learn equally from both modalities, despite the misalignment.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- The paper focuses on semantic misalignment across different modalities in multimodal models and frames this scenario as an “opportunity” to encourage balanced learning across modalities. This approach is very intuitive but effective and proposes an interesting take on what might otherwise be seen as a problem.
- The experiments show consistent improvements compared to previous works, without requiring any modification to the architecture or using external data. Experiments focus on audio/video and image/text dataset, showing the effectiveness of the approach on different combinations of the input modalities.


Weaknesses:
- With MIDAS, the misalignment is created artificially by combining samples with different labels. However, semantic misalignment across modalities is a more broad issue that may arise naturally in data, for example due to annotation errors or intrinsic differences in the semantic information that each modality can encode [1]. In this sense, the proposed approach appears limited as it assumes that samples are normally aligned. Could it be extended to a more realistic scenario in which we don’t know if and how much the individual modalities composing a sample are misaligned? In other words, could MIDAS be applied to already misaligned samples?
- The labeling strategy and the weak-modality weighting mechanism in MIDAS encourage the multimodal classifier to match the confidence of the individual unimodal classifiers. However, different modalities may have different levels of information and convergence rates, making some modalities inherently more “informative” and therefore their respective unimodal classifiers more confident. This also makes the confidence scores not comparable across modalities. Why is the multimodal model expected to match the performance of their unimodal counterparts? Is this optimal? How would this compare with other approaches that “hardcode” the balance between the two modalities (for example giving the two modalities the same weight)?


[1] Von Kügelgen, Julius, et al. "Self-supervised learning with data augmentations provably isolates content from style." NeurIPS 2021

QUESTIONS:
- Sec. 4.4 presents an analysis on the performance of MIDAS on misaligned data but few details are provided on this evaluation. How are the misaligned data obtained in this case? Given the focus of the paper on misaligned data, I think that the evaluation on misaligned data would require more attention. 
- Are the baselines in the Experiments section also trained on the misaligned samples? If not, how would these baselines compare to MIDAS when trained on the same misaligned data? By looking at the first lines in Table 1 and Table 2, it seems the largest improvement is brought by training on misaligned data alone. It would also be useful to compare the same baselines in Table 1 in this setting.
- As the model is trained with a cross entropy loss, is it optimal to use cosine similarity for hard-sample weighting? I think it would be more appropriate to use cosine similarity if the classifier was a cosine classifier, and L2 distance when using cross entropy.

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes MIDAS, a novel data augmentation strategy for imbalanced multimodal learning that leverages semantically misaligned samples instead of aligned ones. Specifically, MIDAS incorporates two weighting mechanisms to address existing challenges: (1) weak-modality weighting, which aims to better exploit underrepresented modalities, and (2) hard-sample weighting, which focuses on handling semantically ambiguous samples. Extensive experiments on multimodal classification tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

(1)	This paper proposes MIDAS, a novel approach that leverages semantically misaligned samples to achieve superior performance in imbalanced multimodal learning.

(2)	The paper is well-written with a clear and logically structured presentation. The results are supported by well-designed figures and tables, which enhance the overall clarity of the work.

(3)	The experimental evaluation is relatively comprehensive, including comparisons with baselines, detailed ablation studies, and additional insightful discussions.

Weaknesses:

(1)	While the experimental evaluation is generally solid, it lacks comparisons with several advanced and competitive baselines (e.g., CGGM [1], Dyncim [2]), which limits a full assessment of the proposed method's advantages.

(2)	The paper would benefit from visualizations illustrating how MIDAS's two weighting mechanisms assign weights across modalities and samples, which could offer deeper insights into the model’s internal behavior.

[1] Guo Z, Jin T, Chen J, et al. Classifier-guided gradient modulation for enhanced multimodal learning[J]. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2024, 37: 133328-133344.

[2] Qian C, Han K, Wang J, et al. Dyncim: Dynamic curriculum for imbalanced multimodal learning[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.06456, 2025.

QUESTIONS:
While the experimental evaluation is generally solid, this paper lacks comparisons with several advanced and competitive baselines, which limits the ability to fully assess the advantages of the proposed method. Moreover, the paper would benefit from visualizations illustrating how MIDAS’s two weighting mechanisms assign weights across modalities and samples, providing deeper insights into the model’s internal behavior. Additionally, it remains unclear whether the authors have explored the impact of alternative strategies—beyond random replacement—for generating misaligned samples on the method’s performance.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces MIDAS (Misalignment-based Data Augmentation Strategy), an approach addressing modality imbalance in multimodal learning. The method generates semantically inconsistent cross-modal samples (e.g., pairing a "cat" image with "dog" text) labeled using unimodal confidence scores, compelling models to learn from conflicting signals. MIDAS incorporates two weighting mechanisms: weak-modality weighting enhances learning of underrepresented modalities through dynamic loss adjustment, while hard-sample weighting prioritizes semantically ambiguous samples to improve discriminative power.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths
1. The use of misaligned samples as supervised training signals, along with dynamic weighting mechanisms, introduces a new perspective.
2. The paper conducts thorough experiments on four multimodal datasets, with results showing improvements over existing methods.
3. The methodology is described systematically, with clear mathematical formulations and supporting diagrams.

Weaknesses
1. The impact of key hyperparameters (e.g., loss weight λ) is not systematically studied.
2. The experimental validation remains limited in scope, failing to include several key benchmark datasets such as Sarcasm, Twitter2015, NVGesture, and VGGSound.
3. Although empirically effective, the paper lacks a theoretical analysis for the proposed weighting schemes, which could deepen understanding of their behavior.
4. While feature-level augmentation is mentioned as an efficiency measure, no quantitative comparison (e.g., training time, memory usage) is provided to support this claim.

QUESTIONS:
Please see weaknesses.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The paper proposes to generate misaligned samples as a data augmentation strategy to handle modality imbalance problem in multimodal learning. Unimodal confidence scores are leveraged to achieve this generation and further a weighting mechanism is introduced to support weaker modalities during learning.

Some notable strengths of the submission include:
- a novel data augmentation strategy to generate misaligned samples 
- thorough experimental results and the paper is well-written

Among weaknesses, the concerning were:
- missing impact on hyperparameters 
- evaluation scope is limited, as some important benchmarks are missing (e.g., Sarcasm, Twitter2015, NVGesture)
- some visualizations are required to better understand the two weighting mechanisms
- grounding of the method that proposed modality balanced actually provides the claimed gains
- assumption of data being normally aligned and what if the data is by default misaligned

After the post-rebuttal discussion phase, majority of the reviewers remained positive about the novelty of the submission and the experimental validation. Some new results such as hyperparameter study, more dataset results, comparison with more recent baselines were provided during the rebuttal and post-rebuttal discussion period. Furthermore, the clarifications on normal aligned data assumption and stability of experimental results were acknowledged by the reviewers. Overall, nothing major or concerning remained after the closing of author-reviewer discussion. Therefore, the AC decides to recommend acceptance.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
